subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Listen to this sound: b-b-b-b. Like a bubble going pop! What letters make that sound?
correct_response: B-b-b-b-ubble! You popped that bubble perfectly! The letters B and B both make a bubbling sound!
incorrect_response: Not quite. B-b-b-b is spelled with two B's. Say them slowly: buh... buh... buh... bubble!
hint: Think of blowing bubbles. The letters are both the same!
mnemonic: B and B both make bubbles - b-b-b-b!
metacognitive_prompt: Can you think of other words that have double letters? Like 'puppy' has two p's?

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: What sound do these letters make together? d-d-d. Like someone going up odd stairs... bump, bump, bump!
correct_response: D-d-d! Great job! Those are both D's! You hear that in words like 'ladder', 'middle', 'paddle' - they all have D-D!
incorrect_response: Not quite. Those are both D's. Listen carefully: d-duh... duh... Try saying them slowly!
hint: Listen for the hard D sound in the middle: d-duh... duh! Like in ladder!
metacognitive_prompt: Can you think of other D-D words you know? Like 'bed', 'kidney'?

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: I see a fluffy white cloud in the sky. What sound do these letters make? f-f-f... fluff!
correct_response: F-f-luff! Exactly right! Those are both F's making a soft sound - like fluff or stuffing!
incorrect_response: Not quite. Those are both F's. One F is at the front for 'ffff', and another is at the end. F-f! Listen: fffff... Like a soft wind blowing!
hint: Think of something soft and white... clouds, fluff pillows, feathers. What letters make that soft sound?
mnemonic: F-F makes soft sounds like fluff!
metacognitive_prompt: Can you think of soft things that start with F-F?

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: What yellow fruit has this letter at the start? L-l... lemon, light!
correct_response: L-l-y-ll-light! Yes! Lemon has double L! Yellow lemons are so sunny and bright with their L-L-Y-L letters!
incorrect_response: Nearly! The yellow fruit starts with L... L... L! Listen: L-L-Yellow. There are two L's in 'll', so it starts with double L!
hint: Think of the letter L at the start. It's the same letter you say in 'lemon'!
mnemonic: L-L-Y-L makes yellow fruits like lemons!
metacognitive_prompt: Can you name other yellow fruits with double L? Like 'apple', 'jelly'?

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: What makes this hissing sound? S-s-s... like a snake or train! What letters?
correct_response: S-s-s-s-perfect! That's right! You're a hissing champion! The letters S and S make a slithery hissing sound!
incorrect_response: Not quite. Listen carefully: sssss... like a snake! Hiss! The S's and S together make a slithery hiss. Try hissing like you're a snake!
hint: Press your tongue behind your top teeth and make the S sound. Sssss... it's like a snake!
mnemonic: S and S together make hissing sounds!
metacognitive_prompt: Can you make the hissing sound? What does S and S make together?

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Listen to this word: R-A-I-N. The A and I say 'ay' and they're separated by the letter I. What word is it?
correct_response: Rain! You got it! R-A-I-N is a split digraph! The I and A say their own sounds, then come together to make the word!
incorrect_response: Not quite. It's R-A-I-N! The I is the vowel 'ih' and A says 'ay'. Together they make the AY sound in 'rain'!
hint: Think of a long 'aaa' sound... ay! What word has I and A making that ay sound?
metacognitive_prompt: What helped you identify R-A-I-N? Did you notice the two different vowel sounds?

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: What word is this? T-A-I-L. The A and I are together at the end. Like the animal with a long tail!
correct_response: Tail! T-A-I-L again! You're getting good at split digraphs! A says 'ay' and I says 'ih', and together they say AYE... tail!
incorrect_response: Not quite. It's T-A-I-L like in 'rain'. The A is the vowel 'ih' that says 'ay', and the I makes the 'eye' sound. Think: tail!
hint: Think about the part of an animal that wags behind it. T-a-i-l says tail!
mnemonic: A and I make 'ay' and 'eye' sounds!
metacognitive_prompt: How did you know it's T-A-I-L? Can you say the two vowel sounds?

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: I'm hungry for a snack! What do you eat? D-A-Y. Day, play, tray!
correct_response: Day! D-A-Y is a split digraph! The A says 'ay' and Y says 'eye'. Together they make 'aye' sound like 'day'!
incorrect_response: Nearly! It's D-A-Y like in 'play'. The A says 'ay' and Y says 'ee'. Think of day... what did you do today?
hint: Think about what happens when the sun goes down. D... a... yee... day!
mnemonic: A and Y together make 'ay' sound!
metacognitive_prompt: Can you think of other A-Y words? Like 'play', 'say', 'may'?

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: What says 'ay' in this word? P-L-A-Y... like 'play'. Can you hear the A and Y?
correct_response: Play! You heard it! P-L-A-Y is a split digraph! The A says 'ay' and Y says 'eye'. Together they make the 'ay' sound you hear in 'play'!
incorrect_response: Not quite. The A says 'ay' and Y says 'ee'. Think: what comes after A in 'play'? It's Y for... YAY! Play!
hint: Listen for the long 'ay' sound... ay... eee... what comes after it?
mnemonic: A and Y together make 'ay' sound!
metacognitive_prompt: Can you think of other A-Y words? Like 'play', 'say', 'may'?

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: What's long? The long vowel says 'A'! Like in 'name' or 'game' or 'plane'. What word has this sound?
correct_response: Name! The 'A' says 'ay' and the 'ame' is silent! It just sounds like the word 'name'!
incorrect_response: Not quite. The 'A' in 'name' is a split digraph, not a long A like in 'train'. The 'A' says 'ay' and 'me' says 'uh'. Two different sounds. But the word still has a long 'ay' sound from the A. Try saying it: nay... uh... nay... me!
hint: Break it into sounds: N-A... Y... A... Y... M... uh. Can you hear the long 'ay' at the end?
metacognitive_prompt: What helped you identify the 'ay' as long? Did you notice the two vowel sounds?

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: What word has this sound at the end? B-EE... bee, tree, three, free! The E is silent like the letter in 'bee'!
correct_response: Tree! That's right! The 'ee' in 'tree' is a split digraph! Tree starts with 'T' but says 'ee'. You found the silent E!
incorrect_response: Not quite. The word has 'ee' at the end but it's silent. Think of a tree... T-R-double-E-E. The E is sneaky! You can't hear it but it's there!
hint: Think of something with three things... tree, bee, three! What ends with a silent E?
mnemonic: Silent E at end in 'bee' and 'tree'!
metacognitive_prompt: What helped you remember the silent E? Did you think of other EE words like 'bee'?

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: What green vegetable has this sound at the end? P-E-A-S. Beans, peas, lime!
correct_response: Peas! You've got a green thumb! P-E-A-S is a split digraph! The E says 'ee' and A says 'ay'. Together they make the 'ay' sound you hear in 'peas'!
incorrect_response: Not quite. The word has 'ee' at the end but it's silent. Think of growing plants... P-E-double-E-A. The E is sneaky! You can't hear it but it's there!
hint: Think of the word 'green'... P-E-A-S! What's at the end?
mnemonic: Silent E at end in 'peas' and 'beans'!
metacognitive_prompt: What helped you remember the silent E? Did you think of other EE words like 'bee'?

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: What small animal says 'moo'? A cow! The word has this sound at the end! M-O-O!
correct_response: Moo! You're a sound detective! The word ends with 'oo' but has two O's in 'moo'. Cows say moo when they're happy or sad!
incorrect_response: Not quite. The word 'moo' has double O at the end. Listen: m-o-o... mooooo! Like a cow saying it's hungry!
hint: Think of a farm animal... black and white... makes the 'moo' sound!
mnemonic: Moo has two O's - look at the shape!
metacognitive_prompt: Can you think of other OO words? Like 'zoo', 'too', 'moon'?

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: What do we see in the street? S-T-R-E-E-T. Street, meet, feet! What letters do we see?
correct_response: Street! You saw that! S-T-R-E-E-T is a split digraph! The E is silent at the end - just like in 'meet' and 'feet'!
incorrect_response: Not quite. The word has 'ee' at the end but it's silent. Think of walking on... S-T-double-R-E-E. The E is sneaky! You can't hear it but it's there!
hint: Look at the letters... S-T-R... E-E-T. What's at the end?
mnemonic: Silent E at the end in 'street'!
metacognitive_prompt: What helped you see the silent E? Did you notice other EE words like 'bee'?

subject: spelling
read_aloud: What round thing has this sound? O-A... B-O-A-T. Boat, coat, goat, soap, float!
correct_response: Boat! O-A is a split digraph! The O says 'oh' and A says 'ay'. Together they make the 'oat' sound in 'boat'!
incorrect_response: Not quite. The word is 'boat'. It starts with B and ends with 'oat'. Think of a ship floating on water!
hint: What floats on water and has an O-A sound?
metacognitive_prompt: Can you think of words with O-A? Like 'coat', 'soap', 'float'?

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: Listen to this sound: bl... bl... What is that word? I see a blue sky!
correct_response: Blue! Beautiful! You spotted that! B-L is a blend! The B and L together make a 'blue' sound - like sky on a sunny day!
incorrect_response: Not quite. Try other words with 'bl'... clue... flue, glue, clue, clue! B-L is a blend!
hint: Think of the color of the sky on a clear day!
mnemonic: B and L blend makes blue!
metacognitive_prompt: Can you think of other words that start with B-L? Like 'block', 'floor', 'cloud'?

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: What about when it gets dark? C-L-A-C-K! Clock, black, sack, lack!
correct_response: Black! Perfect! C-L-A-C-K is a blend! When letters get together, they make new sounds!
incorrect_response: Not quite. Think of night time... a dark sky, black clouds. C-L-A-C-K! Like in 'clock' or 'sack' or 'lack' - they all start with C!
hint: Think of nighttime things... darkness, clocks, sacks!
metacognitive_prompt: What helped you identify C-L-A-C-K? Did you think of 'clock', 'sack', 'lack'?

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: I see something very small on the grass. What is it? G-R... it's green! Grass, bug, leaf!
correct_response: Grass! G-R... it's a blend! The G and R together make a 'grass' sound - like in the garden!
incorrect_response: Not quite. Try other G-R words... green, grow, grow, groove, ground. G-R is a blend!
hint: Think of the garden... what grows small and green?
mnemonic: G and R blend makes grass!
metacognitive_prompt: Can you think of other G-R words? Like 'grows', 'green', 'grove'?

subject: Spelling
read_aloud: I hear a sound but I can't see the letter! K-N... O... knock! It's silent in 'know'. What word is that?
correct_response: Know! You found the silent K! When K comes before N, it stays quiet. Like in 'know' or 'knee' - the K is silent at the start!
incorrect_response: Not quite. The word is 'know'. Listen: k... n... oh... no! K says 'n' or 'oh' but the K is shy. You can't hear or see it!
hint: Think of knocking... k-n... ock! Who's there?
mnemonic: K is shy and silent at the start of know and knee!
metacognitive_prompt: Can you think of other KN words? Like 'name', 'knee'?
